main_summary.summary: Carolyn Miller and James Miller, interviewed jointly by Emilye Crosby on December 4, 2015, at Mississippi Cultural Crossroads in Port Gibson, recount growing up in Claiborne County, Mississippi, during the 1950s and 1960s and their entry into the local civil rights movement. James was born at the Alcorn State University infirmary in 1953; Carolyn was raised by her paternal grandparents in Hermanville, where her grandmother Lulu Bell Jennings Turnipseed taught school and her grandfather farmed several hundred acres. Both describe paternalistic but functional relationships between black families and white figures of authority, including attorney Alvin Binder, who helped Carolyn access VA mental-health interventions for her Korean War veteran father, and Sheriff Dan McCay, who advanced money toward James's Alcorn tuition. They discuss the black middle class and educator tradition centered on Addison High School, Fair Street as a thriving black commercial and music district, and James's traumatic childhood encounter with a white family that evoked Emmett Till. The interview turns to the movement's arrival in Port Gibson around 1966, organizing by Rudy Shields and the local NAACP youth chapter led by George Henry Walker, the Deacons for Defense's armed protection of the boycott, mass meetings at First A.M.E. Church, and the showdown between Charles Evers's NAACP and the white-installed Bi-Racial Committee that produced the lawsuit NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co.
key_themes: Claiborne County black middle class and educator tradition
Paternalistic cross-racial relationships in segregated Mississippi
Rudy Shields and grassroots NAACP organizing
Deacons for Defense and the 1966 Port Gibson boycott
Veteran experience and intergenerational movement awareness
main_summary.historical_significance:
The interview adds to the Port Gibson and Claiborne County record at the level of street-and-storefront granularity that broader Mississippi movement histories often omit: the layout of Fair Street's black businesses, the specific personalities of organizers like Rudy Shields and George Henry Walker, the mechanics of the Deacons' enforcement of the boycott, and the contested terrain of the Bi-Racial Committee. James Miller's recollection of Alvin Binder's behind-the-scenes role in school construction and county politics, and Carolyn Miller's account of her family's encounter with VA mental-health services through Binder's intervention, document the local-attorney intermediary role that shaped how the Movement operated in small Mississippi towns.

As a joint interview between two participants who entered the movement as youth (Carolyn through the NAACP youth chapter with her grandmother; James through observation and family connections), the transcript captures how children processed events like the assassinations of Emmett Till and Martin Luther King Jr. and how mass meetings at First A.M.E. Church functioned as collective experience. The interview's value as a primary source for the NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co. boycott litigation context is partially attenuated by the cross-contamination penalty and Pass 1 partial-read residual recorded in the manifest.

chapters.title: Born at the Alcorn Infirmary
chapters.summary: James Miller recalls being the only one of his siblings born in a hospital, at the Alcorn State infirmary in 1953 because his mother had a difficult pregnancy, in a community where a midwife named Jessie delivered most of the babies.

chapters.title: Growing Up in a Segregated Society
chapters.summary: He describes a carefree boyhood on the Alcorn campus that changed when the movement arrived and gave him something to focus on, in a society where white folks ran everything but poor Black and poor white families sometimes built quiet one-to-one coalitions, his own father a World War II veteran of the European theater.

chapters.title: Paternalism and the Sharecropper's Bargain
chapters.summary: James reflects on the paternalistic bonds his father held with powerful white men who lent him money and never scolded James for joining the movement, and on the manhood his father compromised, jumping through hoops, to support his family, threaded through with questions of class within the Black community.

chapters.title: The Interwoven White Power Structure
chapters.summary: He explains how the same white men ran city hall, the courthouse, the school board, the banks, and the stores, why that interlocking power made the boycott of the merchants also a boycott of the politicians, and how the daily denial of being called Mr. and Mrs. came down to respect.

chapters.title: Carolyn's Upbringing in Hermanville
chapters.summary: Carolyn Miller describes a more middle-class childhood in Hermanville, raised by grandparents, her grandmother a longtime schoolteacher and her grandfather a farmer who built up some eight hundred acres and Black workers through hard work alone, earning a different kind of standing with whites.

chapters.title: A Sheltered Farm Childhood
chapters.summary: She recalls a self-sufficient farm where the family ate everything they grew and neighbors lived a mile apart, where she never played with white children and was taught by her grandmother that she was just as good as anyone, until middle school brought her to Port Gibson and her parents' separation.

chapters.title: Fathers Who Went to War
chapters.summary: Both Millers trace their fathers' military service, a World War II and a Korean veteran, and how men who had never traveled returned from overseas to face a democracy that would not let them participate, an experience that quietly empowered a generation and that the movement used to expose America's hypocrisy.

chapters.title: Shell Shock and a Lawyer's Humanity
chapters.summary: Carolyn recounts her father returning shell-shocked and schizophrenic, unable to get the care he needed, and the heartbreaking work of restraining him during episodes, helped each time by the white attorney Alvin Binder, whose phone calls she remembers as an act of plain humanity.

chapters.part: School, Fair Street, and a Near-Lynching
chapters.title: All-Black Schools and a Teacher Who Fed the Children
chapters.summary: They describe the all-Black Hermanville school where teachers lived in the community, where Carolyn's grandmother Mrs. Turnipseed fed the hungry children she knew would need it, and where ragged hand-me-down books made clear, only in hindsight, how unequal the schooling was.

chapters.part: School, Fair Street, and a Near-Lynching
chapters.title: A Teacher, a Father, and the Plow
chapters.summary: James, by his own account a troublemaker, remembers his teacher Miss Rachel Wilson walking out to the field where his father was plowing, and his father telling her to do her job and discipline the boy, with the promise that he had her back.

chapters.part: School, Fair Street, and a Near-Lynching
chapters.title: Integration's Hidden Cost
chapters.summary: James argues that the close all-Black school system was strong and that integration came at the expense of Black culture and heritage, assimilated and dropped for what was assumed to be better, even as he holds to education as Malcolm X's passport to the future.

chapters.part: School, Fair Street, and a Near-Lynching
chapters.title: The County Administrator Job
chapters.summary: James recalls coming back to Claiborne County to serve as county administrator, helped into the post by Alvin Binder, and turns to the lesson that Black officials must be held accountable to act in everyone's interest rather than their own, building on trust and mutual respect in a democracy.

chapters.part: School, Fair Street, and a Near-Lynching
chapters.title: Fair Street: A Thriving Black Main Street
chapters.summary: They paint Fair Street, a single block that felt like a carnival every Saturday, lined with thriving Black-owned cafes, juke joints, and grocery stores, with only the fish market in white hands, a place so alive you could hardly walk the sidewalk.

chapters.part: School, Fair Street, and a Near-Lynching
chapters.title: The Blues on Fair Street
chapters.summary: James remembers the blues greats who played the Fair Street joints, Bobby Bland, John Lee Hooker, Muddy Waters, in a thriving community that knew its one block and knew not to wander up to Main Street.

chapters.part: School, Fair Street, and a Near-Lynching
chapters.title: Bumping Into a White Girl
chapters.summary: James tells of running down a lit-up Main Street at Christmas as a boy, accidentally knocking over a little white girl, and her redneck father throwing him to the concrete with a slur and a threat, a brush with death he kept to himself and later understood as his own Emmett Till moment.

chapters.part: School, Fair Street, and a Near-Lynching
chapters.title: From Emmett Till to Tamir Rice
chapters.summary: He draws the line from his own near-killing to the deaths of Emmett Till, Tamir Rice, and Laquan McDonald, and back to his recurring theme that Black leaders placed in authority must be held accountable to those who sacrificed in the movement.

chapters.title: The Killing of Dr. King
chapters.topic: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.
chapters.summary: They recall the assassination of Dr. King rippling through the community, the sky going dark, and Carolyn, a sheltered girl until then, suddenly afraid to get on the school bus or leave the house, learning for the first time to be afraid.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

chapters.title: The Talk, and the Courage of an Open Casket
chapters.summary: Carolyn describes the talk Black families gave their children after King's death about what they could and could not do, and James honors the courage of Mamie Till in showing her son's body and the power of those Jet magazine images to reach every Black household.

chapters.title: A Knife Across the Bridge
chapters.summary: James recounts tense, sometimes violent encounters walking home across the bridge with students from the white military academy, including a night a thrown object and a fight left him certain the white boys would kill him.

chapters.title: George Henry and the Youth NAACP
chapters.summary: They describe finding the movement through the NAACP meetings at First African Methodist Episcopal Church, Carolyn's grandmother walking the family miles into town, and a youth chapter under organizer George Henry that trained the brighter kids to canvass and register voters.

chapters.title: The Veterans Who Brought Militancy Home
chapters.summary: James frames Rudy Shields and a whole cadre of returning veterans across World War II, Korea, and Vietnam as men who brought a militant energy and real strategy, including weapons training, back to their communities.

chapters.title: Black Men With Guns
chapters.summary: He recalls the power of imagining twenty-five or thirty armed Black men keeping the peace, and the moment Dan McKay came to the organizers to de-escalate, trading a no-public-carry ordinance for the guns being put away.

chapters.title: Raising Cane on the Boycott-Breakers
chapters.summary: James recounts the dehumanizing but effective discipline of the boycott, including the night a revenuer caught breaking it was whipped with a switch in an alley off crowded Fair Street, a story that circled the Black community for years.

chapters.title: The Boycott and How It Held
chapters.summary: They explain the boycott of the white merchants who were also the politicians, how the Deacons quietly watched and listed who entered the stores, and why most people honored it less from fear than from a shared seriousness about the atrocities around them.

chapters.title: Watching a Leader Materialize
chapters.summary: James describes a big church meeting where Rudy Shields drew a sharp question that knocked back a reluctant bourgeois man, then called him up front, and from that moment watched a community leader materialize before his eyes.

chapters.title: Rudy Shields, the Chameleon Organizer
chapters.summary: They describe Rudy Shields, an unremarkable-looking man from out of state by way of Chicago, as a genuine and gifted organizer who got things done through other people, empowering the local community in a model James calls beautiful for community development.

chapters.part: Claiborne County and the Mass Meetings
chapters.title: The Sophisticated White Response
chapters.summary: James argues that white Claiborne County put the open-Klan model in check and answered the movement institutionally, infiltrating the boards and parties, organizing lawyers, building a segregation academy, and assembling a biracial committee of handpicked Black members to keep separate-but-equal in place.

chapters.part: Claiborne County and the Mass Meetings
chapters.title: The Showdown at First AME
chapters.summary: They recall the charged meeting at First AME where the white-backed Human Relations Committee, Charles Evers and the NAACP, and Rudy Shields each pressed competing aims, James voicing respect for Evers's bluntness, and connect it to the boycott case that became NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware.

chapters.part: Claiborne County and the Mass Meetings
chapters.title: Rudy Shields Takes the Gun
chapters.summary: They describe the most profound thing they saw Rudy Shields do, walking up to an armed man named Davis and taking the gun from his hand, and the face-to-face confrontations in which Black men spoke to white men as equals, something that, in that time, could get a man killed.

chapters.part: Claiborne County and the Mass Meetings
chapters.title: Mass Meetings at First AME
chapters.summary: They close on the mass meetings at the overflowing First AME, people crowded outside the open windows, Deacons standing guard, and freedom songs pouring from the rafters, James grateful to have lived in revolutionary times.

loc_video.caption: Carolyn Miller and James Miller oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Port Gibson, Mississippi, 2015 December 04
